title: He Kākano Ahau: I Am a Seed - Exploring Ngāti Kahungunu Identity Through Our Awa
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Mangakōtukutuku Stream - the lifeblood of our local hapū. Students will explore how this awa has shaped Ngāti Kahungunu identity, settlement patterns, and community relationships from traditional times to modern environmental challenges.
learningObjectives: Analyze how the Mangakōtukutuku Stream has influenced Ngāti Kahungunu settlement patterns and community development over time
Investigate contemporary environmental challenges facing our awa using mātauranga Māori and scientific perspectives
Collaborate with local kaumātua to document oral histories and traditional knowledge about the stream's significance
Develop and present a community action plan addressing one identified issue affecting the Mangakōtukutuku Stream

activities.title: Whakapapa o te Awa - Mapping Our Connection
activities.description: Students create digital story maps tracing the Mangakōtukutuku Stream's path through our rohe, identifying historical pā sites, traditional mahinga kai areas, and significant cultural landmarks along its banks

activities.title: Kōrero Tuku Iho - Listening to Our Kaumātua
activities.description: Small groups conduct recorded interviews with local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu, focusing on traditional stories, historical events, and cultural practices connected to the Mangakōtukutuku Stream

activities.title: Mātai Taiao - Stream Health Investigation
activities.description: Field trip to conduct water quality testing using both scientific methods (pH, nitrate levels) and mātauranga Māori indicators (presence of native species, water clarity) at three different points along the stream

activities.title: Whakaaro Rua - Dual Perspective Analysis
activities.description: Students compare and contrast Western scientific data with mātauranga Māori knowledge to develop a holistic understanding of the stream's current health and historical changes

activities.title: Mahere Mahi - Community Action Planning
activities.description: Groups develop and present practical action plans for addressing one identified issue, incorporating both cultural and scientific approaches to stream restoration and protection

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated environmental management plans
Local kaumātua knowledge holders from Te Taiwhenua o Tamatea
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring tools and data
Digital mapping software and oral history recording equipment

tasks: Digital story map presentation showing cultural and historical connections to the awa
Written comparative analysis of traditional and scientific perspectives on stream health
Oral presentation of community action plan to panel including kaumātua and local council representatives

nzcAlignment: Identity, Culture, and Organization: Understand how cultural practices vary but reflect similar purposes
Place and Environment: Understand how people interact with natural and cultural environments
Continuity and Change: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
The Economic World: Understand how people participate in economic activities and make choices to meet their needs
